title: Confounding Variables in Risk Data
text: Simpson’s paradox occurs trend appears aggregated data reverses disappears data split confounding variable. Risk data especially vulnerable : Exposure varies subgroup: everyone faces hazard equally Susceptibility varies subgroup: Age, genetics, occupation change vulnerability Reporting conflates subgroups: single “micromorts per year” figure averages across vastly different populations result: population-average micromort value may describe nobody accurately.

what: 2. Flagship Example: Bed Falls (Age as Confounder)
title: Confounding Variables in Risk Data
text: Falling bed kills ~450 Americans per year (CPSC). population average 1.36 micromorts/year. age massive confounder — CDC age-stratified data reveals 2,500-fold difference: 85-year-old man, going bed carries ~10 micromorts — comparable : Riding motorcycle 60 miles (10 micromorts/trip) single ecstasy dose (13 micromorts/dose) day skiing (0.7 micromorts/day) repeated 14 times someone 65, activity carries 0.004 micromorts per night — essentially zero. population average 1.36/year describes neither group accurately. Age confounds bed fall risk two pathways: Fragility: Older adults lower bone density, slower reflexes, higher complication rates identical falls Bed type environment: Hospital beds, care home beds, medication-induced drowsiness increase fall frequency older populations Neither pathway captured aggregate statistic.

title: Confounding Variables in Risk Data
text: Bee wasp stings kill 72 Americans per year (CDC MMWR). population rate 0.22 micromorts/year. nearly fatalities among ~1% venom allergy (JACI, 2015): confounder (allergy status) binary creates extreme bimodal distribution. population average — 0.22 micromorts/year — meaningless groups. Cattle kill 22 Americans per year (CDC MMWR). Population rate: 0.07 micromorts/year. exposure concentrated among ~2.9 million cattle workers: ’s 100-fold difference. Occupation confounder: determines exposure frequency (daily cattle handling vs never) risk magnitude (confined spaces, agitated animals, kick zones). Lightning kills 28 Americans per year (NOAA). Population rate: 0.08 micromorts/year. outdoor agricultural workers face ~15x risk: confounders occupation behaviour: time spent outdoors, open fields, near tall objects, storm season. Drowning kills ~4,000 Americans per year (CDC). population rate ~12 micromorts/year. risk distribution bimodal: Age sex strong confounders. Setting matters : swimming pools (children), natural water (adults), bathtubs (elderly, alcohol-related) distinct risk profiles aggregate hides.

title: Confounding Variables in Risk Data
text: confounding variable must satisfy two conditions: Correlated exposure: confounder determines exposed (e.g., farmers exposed cattle; office workers ) Correlated outcome: confounder affects probability death given exposure (e.g., age affects fall mortality; allergy affects sting mortality) encounter population-average risk: Ask “conditional ?” — identify likely confounders (age, sex, occupation, geography, pre-existing conditions) Seek stratified data — government agencies (CDC, CPSC, NOAA) often publish age- sex-stratified breakdowns Calculate conditional rates — use conditional_risk() package compare hedged vs unhedged scenarios Report range, average — range like “0.004–10.2 micromorts/night depending age” informative “1.36 micromorts/year”

title: How Reliable Are These Numbers?
text: Three factors drive disagreement sources: Numerator uncertainty: Death attribution varies coding system (ICD-10 codes, verbal autopsy, hospital records) Denominator uncertainty: many people exposed? “deaths per year” figure means nothing without knowing exposure population Temporal geographic aggregation: global annual average hides enormous regional seasonal variation inclusion criteria: traceable numerator + defined denominator + reproducible calculation. reject risks identify death count population risk.

title: Introduction to Micromorts and Risk Visualization
text: micromorts measure sudden death, microlives measure impact chronic habits life expectancy. microlife represents 30-minute change life expectancy per day exposure. useful way think microlives: “use ” 48 microlives per day just living (24 hours = 48 × 30 minutes). Unhealthy habits accelerate consumption, healthy habits slow . smoker smokes 20 cigarettes per day uses additional 10 microlives, can interpreted rushing towards death 29 hours per day instead 24. Conversely, someone excellent lifestyle habits might effectively live 22 hours per day. Healthcare bonus: Modern healthcare healthier lifestyles give us “payback” approximately 12 microlives per day — expected death moving away us even age.

title: Introduction to Micromorts and Risk Visualization
Introduction to Micromorts and Risk Visualization
text: Micromorts (acute, per-event risk) microlives (chronic, per-day attrition) measure different phenomena, can approximately converted using expected value theory. Key relationship: 1 micromort ≈ 0.7 microlives Assumptions conversion: 1. Remaining life expectancy = 40 years (use lle(prob, life_expectancy = ...) adjust age; see also daily_hazard_rate()) 2. Death occurs immediately upon event (worst case) 3. Linear approximation (valid small probabilities) Mathematical derivation: 1 micromort = 1/1,000,000 probability death Expected life lost = probability × remaining life = 1e-6 × 40 years = 4e-5 years = 4e-5 × 525,960 minutes/year ≈ 21 minutes 1 microlife = 30 minutes, 21 minutes ≈ 0.7 microlives common_risks() uses microlives = micromorts × 0.7: conversion allows comparing single risky event (like one skydive 8 micromorts) chronic daily habits (like smoking -10 microlives/day). However, approximation breaks : Remaining life expectancy differs 40 years (20-year-old loses per micromort 80-year-old) risk immediate death (injuries, disabilities captured) Repeated exposures compound non-linearly Use micromorts discrete, short-duration events binary outcomes (death survival): surgery, skydiving, single car trip Use microlives chronic, daily habits accumulate lifetime: smoking, exercise, diet Convert policy decisions, state assumptions (age, remaining life expectancy)

title: Introduction to Micromorts and Risk Visualization
text: Value Statistical Life (VSL) monetary value used justify safety spending. value individual life, aggregate willingness pay small risk reductions. Example: safety feature costs $50 saves 1 life 100,000 people (10 micromorts), worth ? Cost per micromort saved = $50 / 10 = $5. VSL = $10M, 1 micromort = $10. Since $5 < $10, cost-effective. Interestingly, two UK government agencies arrive similar valuations micromorts microlives: near-equivalence (£1.60 ≈ £1.70) provides empirical support theoretical conversion: 1 micromort ≈ 1 microlife policy terms. consistency suggests policy decisions affecting acute risks (transport safety) chronic risks (healthcare interventions) can compared common scale.

what: 6. Complementary Metrics: QALY, DALY, and Morbidity
title: Introduction to Micromorts and Risk Visualization
text: Micromorts microlives focus mortality. many conditions (like common cold) cause significant quality life loss without fatal. Complementary metrics capture morbidity burden. Measures years life adjusted quality. 1 QALY = 1 year perfect health. Health states weighted 0 (death) 1 (perfect health) year chronic pain 0.7 quality = 0.7 QALYs Used assess cost-effectiveness medical interventions (e.g., £20,000-30,000 per QALY threshold UK) Measures disease burden sum two components: YLL (Years Life Lost): premature mortality YLD (Years Lived Disability): morbidity, weighted disability severity fatal diseases like COVID-19, YLL dominates. non-fatal conditions like common cold, YLD dominates. Spiegelhalter D (2012). “Using speed ageing ‘microlives’.” BMJ 2012;345:e8223. doi:10.1136/bmj.e8223 Spiegelhalter D (2012). “Understanding uncertainty: Microlives.” Plus Magazine. plus.maths.org Global Burden Disease: ghdx.healthdata.org NICE Methods Guide: nice.org.uk

title: Palatable Units: The Spiegelhalter Philosophy
text: micromort measures acute hazard: immediate probability event causing death. Normalization: Risk normalized per event (per unit distance), independent event’s duration. Skydiving: risk ~7 micromorts per jump. Whether freefall lasts 30 seconds 60 seconds secondary event jumping. Anesthesia: ~10 micromorts per operation. following table uses common_risks(), package’s curated dataset 62 acute risks full provenance tracking: Comparison: Riding motorcycle just 60 miles carries acute death risk (~10 micromorts) undergoing general anesthesia. title: Palatable Units: The Spiegelhalter Philosophy
text: key motivation palatable units correcting perception gap fear actually kills us. According World Data, media coverage dramatically misrepresents actual causes death: Key insight: Heart disease cancer cause 56% deaths receive 7% media coverage. Meanwhile, terrorism (causing 16 deaths 2023) received 18,000× coverage proportional death rate. Micromorts microlives provide standardized currency cut emotional reactions: Terrorism (flying 2001): ~0.01 micromorts per flight Daily baseline (age 40): ~2 micromorts per day Driving 230 miles: 1 micromort fear flying 9/11 led many Americans drive instead, resulting estimated 1,600 additional road deaths—far exceeding attack’s direct toll. news reports “50% increase cancer risk,” use framework: Find baseline: ’s absolute risk? (e.g., 1 10,000) Convert micromorts: 1 10,000 = 100 micromorts Apply increase: 50% = 150 micromorts Compare familiar risks: 150 micromorts ≈ driving 150 × 230 = 34,500 miles contextualization reveals whether “scary” headline represents meaningful risk change.

title: Risk Equivalence Dashboard
text: Atomic vs composite risks. atomic_risks() function returns ONE row per risk component per activity. Activities yet decomposed use component = "all_causes" (honest placeholder indicating breakdown unknown). Conditional risks. components depend health profile (e.g., DVT risk varies whether risk factors). default profile assumes “healthy” values; use common_risks(profile = list(health_profile = "dvt_risk_factors")) alternatives. Geographic conditioning can change equivalences dramatically: snake bite 0.5 mm US 18.5 mm rural Africa (37x). See Data Reliability vignette details. Duration bucketing. Rather encoding rate functions, flight risks pre-computed standard duration buckets (2h, 5h, 8h, 12h). Every number directly citable — hidden formulas. DVT literature. DVT risk 4 hours negligible. 4 hours, risk rises nonlinearly. Compression socks + hydration + movement reduce DVT risk approximately 60–70%. Sources: Lancet Haematology, Cochrane Reviews. Medical radiation. “(radiation)” label indicates radiation dose risk. invasive procedures (e.g., coronary angiogram), procedural risks (infection, bleeding) separate components yet decomposed. Confidence levels. component carries confidence rating: “high” (published meta-analyses), “medium” (single studies expert consensus), “low” (extrapolated), “estimated” (order--magnitude).

title: Micromort - Curated Risk Datasets
text: micromort unit mortality risk equal one---million probability death per specific event. Unit: 1 micromort = 1/1,000,000 = 0.0001% death probability Scope: Per discrete event (e.g., one skydive, one surgery, one flight) Sign: Always non-negative (’s probability) Example: Skydiving ~8 micromorts per jump (8---million death chance per jump)

title: Micromort - Curated Risk Datasets
text: microlife unit life expectancy change equal 30 minutes expected lifespan, measured per day exposure. Unit: 1 microlife = 30 minutes life expectancy Scope: Per day maintaining habit exposure Sign: Positive (life gained) negative (life lost) Example: Smoking 2 cigarettes daily costs -1 microlife/day (losing 30 mins life expectancy day smoke)

title: Micromort - Curated Risk Datasets

title: Convert Minutes to Microlives — as_microlife

title: Convert Minutes to Microlives — as_microlife

title: Convert Minutes to Microlives — as_microlife
text: minutes Numeric. Life expectancy change minutes. Positive values = life gained (e.g., exercise) Negative values = life lost (e.g., smoking)

title: Convert Minutes to Microlives — as_microlife
text: Numeric. Value microlives (sign input).

title: Convert Minutes to Microlives — as_microlife
text: Unit definition: 1 microlife = 30 minutes life expectancy change per day. Sign convention: Negative microlives = life expectancy loss (harmful) Positive microlives = life expectancy gain (beneficial)

title: Convert Minutes to Microlives — as_microlife
code: # Smoking 20 cigarettes/day: each costs ~30 mins = -600 mins total as_microlife(-20 * 30)  # -20 microlives (life lost) #> [1] -20  # Exercise 20 mins/day: gains ~60 mins life expectancy as_microlife(60)        # +2 microlives (life gained) #> [1] 2  # Being 5kg overweight: costs ~30 mins/day as_microlife(-30)       # -1 microlife (life lost) #> [1] -1

what: Prepare Risk Data for Plotting — prepare_risks_plot
title: Prepare Risk Data for Plotting — prepare_risks_plot
text: Filters prepares risk data visualization. Use filter categories passing plot_risks().

title: Prepare Risk Data for Plotting — prepare_risks_plot
code: prepare_risks_plot(   risks = common_risks(),   categories = NULL,   exclude_categories = NULL,   min_micromorts = 0.1,   top_n = NULL )

title: Prepare Risk Data for Plotting — prepare_risks_plot
text: risks Tibble. Dataframe risks, defaults common_risks(). categories Character vector. Categories include. Use NULL (default) categories. See common_risks() available categories. exclude_categories Character vector. Categories exclude. Applied categories filter. min_micromorts Numeric. Minimum micromorts include (default 0.1 avoid invisible bars log scale). top_n Integer. specified, return top N risks micromorts.

title: Generate Quiz Pairs for
text: Creates candidate question pairs common_risks() use interactive risk comparison quiz. pair contains two activities similar micromort values, making comparison challenging educational.

title: Generate Quiz Pairs for
code: quiz_pairs(   min_ratio = 1.1,   max_ratio = 2,   prefer_cross_category = TRUE,   seed = NULL )

title: Generate Quiz Pairs for
text: min_ratio Minimum ratio micromort values pair. Values 1.0 exclude identical-risk pairs unanswerable. Default 1.1. max_ratio Maximum ratio micromort values pair. Lower values produce harder questions. Default 2.0. prefer_cross_category TRUE (default), pairs different risk categories prioritised -category pairs. seed Optional random seed reproducibility.

title: Generate Quiz Pairs for
text: tibble columns: activity_a, micromorts_a, category_a, hedgeable_pct_a, period_a activity_b, micromorts_b, category_b, hedgeable_pct_b, period_b description_a, help_url_a, description_b, help_url_b ratio (max/min two micromort values) answer ("" "b" — whichever activity riskier)

title: Generate Quiz Pairs for

title: Radiation Exposure Profiles — radiation_profiles
text: Compares annual cumulative radiation exposure across occupational, passenger, environmental profiles. Uses Linear -Threshold (LNT) model dose--risk conversion.

title: Radiation Exposure Profiles — radiation_profiles
text: milestones Integer vector career/exposure years cumulative columns. Default c(10, 20, 40).

title: Radiation Exposure Profiles — radiation_profiles
text: tibble columns: activity_id, activity, category, annual_msv, annual_micromorts, milestone columns (mm_Ny N), regulatory_limit_msv, xray_equivalents_per_year.

what: Regional Life Expectancy in Western Europe — regional_life_expectancy
title: Regional Life Expectancy in Western Europe — regional_life_expectancy
text: Life expectancy birth NUTS2 region Western European countries, based Eurostat data methodology Bonnet et al. (2026).

title: Regional Life Expectancy in Western Europe — regional_life_expectancy
code: regional_life_expectancy(   country = NULL,   year = NULL,   sex = NULL,   classification = NULL )

title: Regional Life Expectancy in Western Europe — regional_life_expectancy
text: country Character vector. Filter specific countries using ISO 2-letter codes (e.g., "FR", "DE", "ES"). Default NULL returns countries. year Integer vector. Filter specific years. Default NULL returns years (1992-2023). sex Character. Filter sex: "Male", "Female", "Total". Default NULL returns . classification Character. Filter region classification: "vanguard", "average", "laggard". Default NULL returns .

title: Regional Life Expectancy in Western Europe — regional_life_expectancy
text: tibble columns: region_code NUTS2 region code (e.g., "FR10" Île-de-France) region_name Human-readable region name country_code ISO 2-letter country code year Data year (1992-2023) sex Sex category: "Male", "Female", "Total" life_expectancy Life expectancy birth years microlives_vs_eu_avg Daily microlives difference vs EU average classification "vanguard", "average", "laggard" based 2019 trends source_url DOI link source publication

title: Regional Life Expectancy in Western Europe — regional_life_expectancy
text: row represents one region-year-sex combination, individual survey responses. example, dataset 450 regions × 28 years × 3 sex categories = 37,800 rows aggregated statistics. underlying Eurostat data represents ~400 million people across Western Europe. Life expectancy calculated official death registrations census population counts—sample survey.

title: Regional Life Expectancy in Western Europe — regional_life_expectancy
text: Primary data Eurostat dataset demo_r_mlifexp. Regional classifications based Bonnet et al. (2026) methodology identifying: Vanguard regions: Top 20% life expectancy sustained gains (≥1.5 months/year) Laggard regions: Bottom 20% life expectancy stagnant gains (<0.5 months/year) Average regions: others

title: Regional Life Expectancy in Western Europe — regional_life_expectancy
text: microlives_vs_eu_avg column converts life expectancy differences daily microlives using approximation: 1 year LE difference ≈ 1.2 microlives/day (assuming 40 years remaining life expectancy). Example: region +2 years EU average = +2.4 microlives/day, equivalent benefit 20 minutes daily exercise.

title: Regional Life Expectancy in Western Europe — regional_life_expectancy
text: IMPORTANT: Regional life expectancy reflects population averages, individual-level causation. High life expectancy "vanguard" regions results multiple factors including: Healthcare system quality access Socioeconomic composition (income, education) Selection effects (healthy/wealthy people moving certain regions Historical cultural factors Moving high-LE region guarantee increased personal longevity.

title: Regional Life Expectancy in Western Europe — regional_life_expectancy
text: Bonnet F, et al. (2026). "Potential challenges sustainable progress human longevity." Nature Communications 17, 996. doi:10.1038/s41467-026-68828-z Eurostat (2024). Life expectancy age, sex NUTS 2 region (demo_r_mlifexp). https://ec.europa.eu/eurostat/databrowser/product/view/demo_r_mlifexp

title: Regional Mortality Multiplier — regional_mortality_multiplier

text: country Character vector. Filter specific countries using ISO 2-letter codes (e.g., "FR", "DE", "ES"). Default NULL returns countries. year Integer vector. Filter specific years.
